title: Death rate from road injuries
subtitle: The annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people. Deaths include those from drivers and passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians.
caption:
Death rate from road injuries, 2021

The annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people. Deaths include those from drivers and passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists and pedestrians.

This choropleth map displays the annual number of deaths from road injuries per 100,000 people across the globe for the year 2021, with a color scale ranging from light beige for the lowest rates to dark red for the highest. The legend defines bands starting at 0 and increasing in increments of 10 up to 70 deaths per 100,000 people, while hatched areas indicate no available data. The highest values appear concentrated in specific regions, with North Korea falling into the darkest red band, indicating a rate above 60 deaths per 100,000 people. Algeria and several nations in West Africa, such as Liberia and Côte d'Ivoire, sit in the orange bands representing rates between 30 and 50. In contrast, large portions of the map show significantly lower mortality figures. Most of Western and Northern Europe, including the United Kingdom, France, Germany, and the Nordic countries, along with Australia and Canada, fall into the lightest beige category, corresponding to rates between 0 and 10 deaths per 100,000 people. The United States and Brazil occupy a middle ground in the light orange band, showing rates between 10 and 20. Greenland is marked with hatching to show that no data is reported for that territory. The data encompasses fatalities among all road users, including drivers, passengers, motorcyclists, cyclists, and pedestrians, providing a comprehensive view of road safety outcomes by country. The geographic distribution shows a clear divide, with the lowest death rates clustered in high-income nations across Europe and Oceania, while the highest rates are visible in parts of Africa and East Asia.
